description: Post-observation coaching questions mapped to F2 framework items. Questions are level-aware and triggered when an item scores 1 or 2. Appraiser selects questions for the debrief conversation.

induction: Walk me through how you decided on the timing for each phase today.
Which part of the lesson felt most rushed — and what would you change?
developing: How did you sequence this lesson to build toward the objective? Where did the pacing break down?
If you taught this lesson again tomorrow, what would you restructure and why?
proficient: When you noticed the pacing shift, what real-time adjustments did you make — and how did you decide?
How do you typically diagnose when a lesson structure isn't serving the class?
lead: How do you coach colleagues to plan for pacing and transitions? What's the hardest habit to shift?
What does your department's lesson planning template do well — and what gap does this lesson expose?

D1.2.title: Clarity and Measurability of Learning Objectives

induction: If I asked a student right now what they were supposed to learn today, what would they say?
How would you rewrite today's objective so a student could measure their own success?
developing: What success criteria were students working toward — and how did you communicate them?
How do you decide whether an objective is measurable enough before the lesson?
proficient: How did you check that students understood the objective — not just heard it?
When objectives are fuzzy, how does that affect the rest of your planning decisions?
lead: How do you ensure consistent objective quality across your department's lessons?
What's your approach to coaching teachers who write activity-focused rather than learning-focused objectives?

induction: Looking back at each activity, which ones directly served the objective — and which ones didn't?
How do you check for alignment when you're planning?
developing: There were moments where the activity seemed to drift from the objective. What caused that drift?
How do you stay disciplined about cutting activities that are engaging but misaligned?
proficient: When you sense the class is going off-purpose mid-lesson, what do you do?
How do you design activities that make the alignment visible to students — not just to you?
lead: How do you build alignment-checking into your department's planning routines?
What does misalignment typically look like when you observe colleagues — and how do you address it?

induction: Who in this class needs the most support — and what did you plan specifically for them?
How do you find out what your highest-attaining students already know before you plan?
developing: What differentiation strategies did you use today — and how did you decide on them?
Which students did you feel your plan didn't fully serve, and what would you add?
proficient: How do you move beyond tiered tasks to personalised differentiation at scale?
When differentiation plans don't work in the room, what do you do in the moment?
lead: How do you build shared differentiation knowledge in your department?
What professional learning would most shift your team's differentiation practice?

D1.5.title: Balance of Individual and Collaborative Learning

induction: When you chose individual or group work today, what was your reasoning?
How do you know when a task is better done alone versus together?
developing: What learning goals are best served by collaboration in your subject? How did that show in today's plan?
When collaborative work doesn't produce the outcome you hoped for, what usually goes wrong?
proficient: How do you design group tasks so every individual is accountable — not just the group?
What's your evidence that collaborative tasks in your lessons produce deeper learning?
lead: How do you lead a department-wide conversation about when collaboration adds value versus when it's a default habit?

induction: What criteria do you use when choosing materials for a lesson?
Were there any materials today that didn't quite fit — what would you replace them with?
developing: How do you evaluate whether a resource is doing the teaching job, or just filling time?
What would a better-matched resource for today's objective have looked like?
proficient: How do you build a repertoire of materials that you know work for specific learning goals?
When materials are weak, how do you adapt them rather than replace them?
lead: How do you curate and share high-quality materials across the department?
What's your process for auditing whether the materials your team is using are fit for purpose?

induction: At what point in today's lesson would you have known whether students understood the key concept?
What's one formative check you could add to this lesson plan?
developing: How do you embed check-for-understanding moments that don't slow the lesson down?
When formative data tells you students haven't got it, what happens next?
proficient: How varied are your formative assessment strategies across a week of lessons?
How do you use formative data to make in-flight adjustments — not just to plan tomorrow?
lead: How do you build formative assessment into your department's planning norms?
What does quality formative practice look like at each level in your team?

induction: Which part of the room are you least aware of during independent work — and why do you think that is?
What signals do you look for to know a student is about to go off-task?
developing: How do you maintain 'with-it-ness' when you're working intensively with one group?
Walk me through what happened when [specific behaviour] occurred. What did you notice and when?
proficient: How do you proactively prevent off-task behaviour rather than respond to it?
What does your movement pattern through the room look like — is it systematic or reactive?
lead: How do you coach colleagues who have blind spots in their classroom awareness?

induction: How would you describe the atmosphere in your classroom today? What caused that?
What does a purposeful learning environment look and sound like to you?
developing: When the atmosphere shifted today, what triggered it — and how did you respond?
Which students feel most safe to take risks in your class, and which ones don't? What creates that difference?
proficient: How deliberately do you build psychological safety — beyond just being 'nice'?
What classroom norms are working well, and which ones need reinforcing?
lead: How do you help colleagues diagnose and rebuild a poor classroom climate?

induction: Which phase ran over time today — and what would you cut if you had to choose?
How do you track time during a lesson without constantly watching the clock?
developing: When a task takes longer than planned, how do you decide what to sacrifice?
What transition moments cost the most time — and what's your fix?
proficient: How do you pace a lesson that has students at different speeds?
What's your internal signal that the lesson is losing momentum?
lead: How do you address time management as a whole-department issue, not just an individual one?

D2.6.title: Managing Learning Activities and Student Behaviour

induction: What expectations did students have before [specific activity]? How were they communicated?
When [behaviour] happened, what did you do — and would you do it differently?
developing: What's your hierarchy of responses to low-level disruption?
How do you handle behaviour without losing the learning thread for the rest of the class?
proficient: How consistent are your expectations day-to-day — and how do students know that?
What's the most common behaviour pattern in this class, and what's your strategy for it?
lead: How do you build a shared behaviour management approach across your department?

induction: Walk me through how you signalled the transition between [phase A] and [phase B].
What would a 60-second transition look like for that activity?
developing: How do you prepare students for a transition before it happens?
Which transitions are easiest for your class, and which are hardest — why?
proficient: How do you teach students to self-manage transitions rather than waiting for your direction?
lead: How do you build consistent transition norms across teachers in your team?

induction: At the end of the lesson, could students tell you what they learned — not just what they did?
How did you communicate the objective — and did you refer back to it?
developing: How do success criteria help students monitor their own progress?
What's the difference between posting an objective and making it meaningful?
proficient: How do you involve students in co-constructing success criteria?
When students lose sight of the objective mid-lesson, how do you bring them back?
lead: How do you ensure every teacher in your department makes learning intentions purposeful — not performative?

induction: For each task today: what learning did it produce, specifically?
Were there any tasks that felt like fillers? What would you replace them with?
developing: How do you 'test' an activity against the objective before you use it?
When tasks go off-purpose, is it a planning issue or an in-the-moment decision?
proficient: How do you build students' ability to understand why they're doing each activity?
lead: How do you review lesson plans with colleagues to catch misalignment early?

induction: What was the hardest thinking students did today — and what made it hard?
Can you point to a moment where a student had to do more than recall something?
developing: How do you take a recall task and turn it into an analysis task?
Which Bloom's levels appeared in today's lesson — and where were the gaps?
proficient: How do you design tasks that require students to produce something original, not reproduce something familiar?
What does student struggle that leads to growth look like in your lessons?
lead: How do you shift your department's default from comprehension to analysis?
What's your approach to coaching teachers who equate 'difficult' with 'higher-order'?

induction: How many different teaching strategies did you use today — can you name them?
What strategy would you add if you taught this lesson again?
developing: How do you decide which strategy fits which content?
What's your strongest strategy — and what's the one you avoid but probably shouldn't?
proficient: How do you know when to change strategy mid-lesson rather than push through?
What's your mental model for matching strategy to cognitive demand?
lead: How do you build a shared strategy repertoire across your department?

induction: How many questions did you ask today — and how many required more than a one-word answer?
What would a better follow-up question to [student response] have looked like?
developing: How do you plan questions in advance, and how do you improvise in the moment?
What's your technique for pushing a student's answer further without telling them the answer?
proficient: How do you use questions to build a whole-class dialogue, not just teacher–student exchanges?
What's the difference between a probing question and a leading question — can you give me an example of each from today?
lead: How do you coach colleagues to plan higher-order questions before the lesson?
What questioning habits are hardest to shift in experienced teachers?

induction: Who answered questions most today — and who barely spoke? Why do you think that happened?
What strategy could you use next lesson to get more voices involved?
developing: How do you use cold-calling, think-pair-share, or random selection — and when?
What do you do when a student you call on says 'I don't know'?
proficient: How do you make all students feel accountable for thinking — not just responding?
What patterns do you notice in who participates and who doesn't?
lead: How do you make equitable participation a team-wide norm?

induction: Which students were disengaged today — and when did you notice?
What's one thing you could do in the first five minutes to bring a disengaged student back in?
developing: How do you re-engage a student without making it feel like a confrontation?
What does 'passive participation' look like in your class — and how do you move students past it?
proficient: How do you design tasks so that opting out is harder than engaging?
What's your diagnostic for a student who is present but not learning?
lead: How do you address participation culture as a systemic issue, not just an individual one?

induction: When you introduced [concept], how did you know students were ready to try it independently?
Walk me through your 'I do / We do / You do' sequence today — did it happen?
developing: How much scaffolding did students need today versus what you planned for?
What's your method for deciding when to release students to independence?
proficient: How do you scaffold without creating dependency?
When students struggle at the independent practice stage, what does that tell you about your guided instruction?
lead: How do you build a department-wide understanding of gradual release?

induction: At what moments today did you check whether students understood — what did you do?
If 30% of your class hadn't understood the main concept, would you have known by the end of the lesson?
developing: How do you gather evidence of understanding in real time — beyond asking 'does everyone get it'?
What did the formative data you collected today tell you — and what will you do with it?
proficient: How do you monitor understanding at the individual level, not just the class level?
What in-flight adjustment did you make today based on what you saw — or what should you have made?
lead: How do you build formative assessment habits across your team so monitoring isn't just the strong teachers' practice?

induction: What feedback did students receive today — and what could they do with it?
Can you describe a piece of feedback you gave that a student could act on immediately?
developing: How do you make feedback specific enough to be useful without making it overwhelming?
What's the difference between feedback and correction — and which did you use more today?
proficient: How do you build students' capacity to seek and use feedback, not just receive it?
What's your approach to written versus verbal feedback — when do you choose each?
lead: How do you make high-quality feedback a consistent expectation across your department?
What feedback culture exists in your team — and how do you model it?

induction: Did students know what a good answer looked like before they started the task?
How would you make the success criteria more student-friendly?
developing: How did you share the criteria — and how did you check students understood them?
What's the difference between handing out a mark scheme and teaching students to use it?
proficient: How do you use exemplars alongside criteria to make quality tangible?
How do you involve students in interpreting or co-creating criteria?
lead: How do you ensure assessment criteria are shared, understood, and consistently applied across your department?

induction: Does today's assessment task show you what students actually learned — or what they can copy?
What would a more revealing assessment task look like for this objective?
developing: How varied are your assessment tasks across a unit — and what's the purpose of each type?
What makes a task 'low ceiling' — and how do you spot that in your own planning?
proficient: How do you design assessments that produce information you can actually use?
lead: How do you review your department's assessment diet for variety and rigour?

developing: How do you track individual student progress beyond grades?
What would you need to be able to say about each student's trajectory at the end of the year?
proficient: How does your tracking system help you have better conversations with students about their growth?
When a student plateaus, how do you use data to identify why?
lead: How do you build a team-wide progress monitoring culture that goes beyond mark books?

proficient: What did your last assessment tell you — and what did you change as a result?
How do you document the adjustments you make based on assessment data?
lead: How do you use departmental assessment data to set team priorities, not just individual ones?
What's your model for leading a data discussion with your department that leads to action — not just analysis?
